text: Hello, this is Roxanne Liu from Oakland, California. This is a video of an elective cholecystectomy for gallstones. Turns out this was a difficult gallbladder because unbeknownst to anybody, she had pus in her gallbladder and a giant stone. She was completely asymptomatic at the time of surgery, so this was not expected. Unfortunately, I had the XI Robot that day.

The surgeon places ports for robotic cholecystectomy, including a Hasson port at the umbilicus for the hook, a camera port at the right midclavicular line, a fenestrated bipolar instrument at the right anterior axillary line, and a fourth robot port at the left midclavicular line for the suction irrigator. The gallbladder appears abnormal, prompting immediate use of the suction irrigator.

The surgeon uses the suction irrigator as a retractor to elevate the liver and gallbladder, revealing a long gallbladder with minimal foreground fat. The Calot's triangle is clearly visible, allowing for easy dissection within the open anatomical space.

The surgeon activates the Firefly imaging system, revealing green fluorescence indicating the common hepatic duct and common bile duct. The Firefly provides enhanced visualization of critical structures, aiding in mapping the dissection path. The surgeon begins to remove superficial peritoneal tissue, noting the proximity of the common hepatic duct to the gallbladder.

The surgeon attempts to achieve the critical view of safety by dissecting fibrotic tissue around the gallbladder. The robotic hook is used to manipulate the tissue, but the fibrotic nature makes it difficult to engage effectively.

The Firefly imaging system illuminates the bile ducts in bright green, enhancing visibility during the dissection of the Calot's triangle. The hepatic artery and its branches are not highlighted by the ICG used pre-operatively, leaving them less visible in the current view.

The surgeon employs a dome-down approach to detach the gallbladder from the liver bed, aiming to pull the gallbladder further away from the liver. This technique is used to maintain distance from critical structures within the fibrotic triangle of Calot, given the gallbladder's length and inflammation.
The surgeon uses the suction irrigator as a retractor to manipulate the gallbladder, noting its tendency to slip off but also its effectiveness in maintaining efficiency during the operation.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to evacuate smoke from the surgical field and accesses the gallbladder to suction out clear hydropic fluid. The combination of suction irrigator and hook enhances efficiency during the operation.

The surgeon activates the Firefly imaging system to visualize the liver and gallbladder junction, providing a clear view of the anatomical structures. Concurrently, the suction device is used to evacuate smoke while serving as a retractor, maintaining a stable image of the surgical field.

When you're in a tough case like this, nothing's more frustrating than when you can't see what you want to see and constantly having to direct your bedside assistant to look at the exact spot you want to look at. So you can see the what I want to see is almost always centered because as I'm dissecting it's just very easy to press the camera clutch pedal so here I get into the gallbladder once in a while but sometimes it's nice to be able to see the lumen to make sure that I am hugging the gallbladder especially as I'm getting closer to the
The surgeon uses the suction irrigator within the cystic triangle to dissect tissue, evacuate smoke, and locate any bleeding vessels.

The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to ensure there is no bile leak while continuing to dissect the gallbladder from the liver. The gallbladder is being carefully separated, with attention to staying close to its lumen, and the dissection is performed at a safe distance from the hepatocystic triangle.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the gallbladder, avoiding the right hepatic duct and artery. Due to distorted anatomy, the surgeon decides to abandon the back wall of the gallbladder and focuses on removing the front. The mucosa of the gallbladder is cauterized, revealing the cystic duct opening, which is then closed with a 3-0 PDS suture. The view resembles that of a traditional laparoscopic technique.

The surgeon uses a hook as a retractor while employing the suction irrigator to lift the liver and remove smoke. The gallbladder, still partially attached, is being dissected on its lateral side. A large gallstone is visible inside the gallbladder. The Firefly imaging is used to identify the liver and gallbladder junction.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the back of the gallbladder, maintaining close proximity to the gallbladder to avoid the common hepatic duct, right hepatic duct, or hepatic artery. The gallbladder is visibly falling away from the liver, providing better exposure. The Firefly imaging is used to visualize the duct structures clearly.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to manipulate fibrotic tissue near the gallbladder, identifying and controlling a bleeding structure with the left hand. The suction irrigator is used to push tissue aside and cauterize with a bipolar device.

The surgeon identifies the cystic artery during the dissection. The gallbladder is being carefully dissected to isolate the cystic duct and cystic artery, while the back wall of the gallbladder remains attached to the surrounding structure.

The surgeon uses suction to clean the surgical field, ensuring no inappropriate use of bipolar cautery. The putative cystic artery is being skeletonized, appearing increasingly like an artery, while the cystic duct is positioned lower. To confirm the identification, the surgeon requests anesthesia to administer two mLs of ICG, enhancing the visibility of the cystic artery.

The surgeon observes the gallbladder positioned far from the hepatocystic triangle, facilitating easier dissection. The cystic artery is illuminated with ICG, confirming its location and ensuring safe distance from the common hepatic duct and right hepatic artery.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers to hug the gallbladder, ensuring close proximity to avoid veering away. The gallbladder exhibits a distinct rind, and the cystic artery is visible nearby.

The surgeon works to achieve the critical view of safety, revealing the cystic artery and surrounding tissue near the cystic duct. The anatomy becomes clearer as the surgeon requests additional ICG to enhance visualization.

The surgeon successfully skeletonizes the cystic artery and prepares to divide it to better isolate the cystic duct for clipping. The cystic artery is illuminated with ICG, initially showing the hepatic artery before the cystic artery comes into view. The arterial structures are close to the dissection site, but the surgeon maintains proximity to the gallbladder, ensuring precise manipulation.
The surgeon clips the cystic artery on the patient's side and uses a hook to divide the specimen side, applying bipolar cautery if bleeding occurs.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to assist in the dissection and employs the Firefly imaging system to guide the skeletonization of the cystic artery. A clip is applied across the cystic artery instead of using a stapler. Another structure, possibly venous, is identified, and a clip is placed between the cystic duct and this structure to ensure no aberrant duct is present.

The surgeon uses Firefly imaging to delineate the cystic duct, enhancing its visibility for dissection. The common hepatic duct is visible to the right, with the cystic duct joining it. The surgeon assesses the length of the cystic duct to ensure adequate space for clip placement.

The surgeon uses Firefly imaging to verify anatomical consistency and check for bile leaks from the liver bed while waiting for the assistant to switch from a hook to clips. The sectionary getter cleans the surgical field. The surgeon uses electrocautery and a hook to divide the cystic duct, ensuring a good length between clips. The hook is replaced with an endocatch, positioned at the umbilical Hassan port for specimen retrieval. Due to the larger stone size, the fascia and skin at the umbilicus are enlarged for easier extraction.
Thank you for watching.
